details.prompt: you are a world class mathematician orchestrating a team to solve olympiad math problems. read claude.md for instructions and solve the problem imo-2026-02.

details.text: I'll start by reading the run state and the problem statement.
This is IMO 2026 Problem 2, a hard geometry problem. Setting up the environment and goal now.
Now I'll fan out three explorers, each scouting a genuinely different route to the problem.

details.final_text:
Report written to /tmp/round-1/math-explorer-similarity.md

Summary: Numerically (via scipy.optimize.fsolve on two independent scalene triangles, filtering for valid regions), I confirmed OM=ON holds across a genuine 1-parameter family of valid (K,L) configurations, and — more strikingly — O appears to slide exactly along the fixed perpendicular bisector of MN as the free parameter varies, suggesting the real target is pinning O to that fixed line rather than a pointwise distance argument. The most promising reformulation is power-of-a-point: OM=ON is equivalent to pow(M,⊙AKL)=pow(N,⊙AKL), and since A lies on the circle, this reduces to comparing secant products MA·MP and NA·NQ along lines AB, AC (numerically confirmed equal to ~1e-9). Direct spiral-similarity/concyclicity guesses (BKLC, MKLN, BNLK, CMKL concyclic; a single spiral similarity at A sending B→C, K→L) were all numerically refuted, so those should not be retried. The crux corpus has no geometry entries yet, so no analogous crux problems could be retrieved; the KB's geometry section offers only generic tools (power of a point, spiral similarity, coordinates/complex) with nothing problem-specific.

Report written to /tmp/round-1/math-explorer-power.md

Summary: The strongest opening is a free, unconditionally-true reduction via the "median of a quadratic form" identity: for O = circumcenter of AKL, OM=ON is exactly equivalent to OB²−OC² = (AB²−AC²)/2, with OA² canceling entirely (verified numerically to machine precision across a solved 1-parameter family of valid (K,L) configurations, where powB−powC stayed exactly constant at (AB²−AC²)/2 even as OM, ON, powB, powC individually varied). Opening 2 pushes this into the power-of-a-point framing via chords BK and CL through ω=circumcircle(AKL), but I confirmed numerically that the second intersection of BK (or CL) with ω is NOT any already-named point (L, N, C, etc.), so that route needs a genuine angle-chase, not a lucky identification. A tempting but false lead (Opening 3) is to treat conditions ∠LBK=∠LNC and ∠LCK=∠BMK as defining full spiral similarities at L and K — I checked the companion side ratios numerically and they do not match, so only the bare angle equalities are usable, which is an important dead-end warning for the outliner. The crux corpus has zero geometry entries, so no analogous past problems are available there; knowledge_base.md's "Synthetic toolkit" entry (power of a point, radical axes, spiral similarity) is the right pointer.

Report written to /tmp/round-1/math-explorer-numeric.md.

Summary: I built a numeric solver for the 1-parameter family (K,L) satisfying the three angle conditions (fixing φ=∠KBA=∠ACL, solving the remaining two angle equations for the ray-distance parameters via fsolve, filtering to solutions that actually satisfy all interior-triangle and interior-angle hypotheses). OM=ON was confirmed to ~1e-15 precision across 14 samples on two structurally different scalene triangles spanning the whole valid φ-range — strong numeric confirmation the statement is true as given. The stronger/more useful reformulation also confirmed: O lies exactly on the perpendicular bisector of MN (not just equidistant, its literal locus is that fixed line) as φ varies continuously — O is not a fixed point. Several natural structural conjectures were tested and **failed** numerically: BKLC concyclic, MKLN concyclic, MK=NL, spiral similarity at A sending B→C,K→L, and circle(AKL) passing through the ABC circumcenter/orthocenter/nine-point center — all flagged as dead ends. The most promising unexplored opening is a power-of-a-point argument via the second intersections of lines AB, AC with circle(AKL), since the hypotheses pair an angle at M with an angle at C (∠BMK=∠LCK) and an angle at N with an angle at B (∠LNC=∠LBK), the classic fingerprint of a spiral-similarity/concyclicity target — I recommend checking circles (BMK) and (CNL), or B,K,N,L-type groupings, which I didn't get to test numerically. Also noted: the crux corpus has zero geometry entries, so no analogous crux problems are available.

Report written to /tmp/round-1/proof-outliner.md

Slugs opened (all in `/home/agentuser/repo/results/imo-2026-02/approaches/`):

- **secant-trig-identity** — reduce OM=ON to c·AP − b·AQ = (c²−b²)/2 via power of B, C along the A-secants, parametrize ω by the tangent-chord angle at A, and prove one trig identity modulo the fully verified decoupled constraint system E1–E6 (the midpoints enter as an exact factor 2 in E3/E4).
- **midpoint-reflection-isogonal** — reflect K in M, L in N: exact dictionary puts K\*, L\* on isogonal rays through A with AK\*=BK, AL\*=CL and transferred angle conditions at M, N; goal becomes BK·AP₁ + AK² − CL·AP₂ − AL² = (c²−b²)/2 along the isogonal chords; gap = the hidden auxiliary circle/spiral map.
- **complex-certificate** — encode conditions 2–3 as Im-vanishing rational equations H₁, H₂ in complex coordinates and exhibit a branch-aware polynomial certificate μG′ = λ₁H₁ + λ₂H₂ (mirror branches falsify naive ideal membership — flagged).
- **family-invariance-boundary** — deformation argument: show F(φ) = pow(B,ω)−pow(C,ω) is constant along the 1-parameter family (⟺ O moves ⊥ BC, via affinity of pow in circle coordinates), then evaluate F at one closed-form boundary member.

While outlining I verified the E1–E6 trig system and the tangent-chord goal form to 1e-15, and refuted ~12 tempting auxiliary claims (listed in the report) — probe script at `/tmp/round-1/scratch/outliner_probe.py`. Recommended build set: `secant-trig-identity, complex-certificate`.

details.text: Four rival approaches are on the table. Now the outline-reviewer ranks the field and emits the build set.

details.final_text:
Report written to /tmp/round-1/outline-reviewer.md

build set: secant-trig-identity, complex-certificate, midpoint-reflection-isogonal

- **secant-trig-identity** — strongest line: the whole reduction chain is verified, and my new adversarial probe (`/tmp/round-1/scratch/reviewer_branch_test.py`, high-precision confirm at 1e-48) shows the load-bearing trig identity holds on all 64 spurious solution branches of E1–E6, so the elimination is branch-free; the only real burden left is deriving E1–E6 rigorously plus one identity proof.
- **complex-certificate** — independent algebraic machinery, but the builder must re-encode via the trig-resolved/branch-pinned forms (plain ideal membership over the Im(·)=0 variety is provably false due to mirror components); with that change my probe indicates the certificate exists over the full variety.
- **midpoint-reflection-isogonal** — the genuinely different (synthetic) framing as the hedge, with a bounded brief: test the two proposed tangent-circle candidates at M/N and record refutations rather than falling back to the trig grind (which would duplicate the leader).

family-invariance-boundary stays live in the population (ranked last, Elo 1454) but is not built: two load-bearing gaps plus the heaviest analytic-rigor burden, and the branch-free identity finding removes its main advantage.

details.text: Build set is three approaches. Dispatching three builders in parallel, one per slug.

details.final_text:
Proof written to `/home/agentuser/repo/results/imo-2026-02/approaches/midpoint-reflection-isogonal.md` (Status: **partial**).

Remaining gaps:
- **Single load-bearing gap:** derive identity (I) — BK·AP₁ + AK² − CL·AP₂ − AL² = (c²−b²)/2 — from the transferred angle conditions ∠AMK\* = ∠LCK, ∠ANL\* = ∠LBK. The bounded candidate hunt mandated by the reviewer came back fully negative: both tangent-circle candidates (Γ₁ through K\* tangent to AB at M, Γ₂ through L\* tangent to AC at N), the direct tangent-chord circles through K/L, all spiral-similarity ratio forms at A, the second meets K₂/L₂ of lines KM/LN with ω, and fixed points of lines K\*L\*/KL — all refuted numerically on two unrelated triangles (probes `/tmp/round-1/scratch/builder_mri_probe.py`, `builder_mri_probe2.py`). Refutations recorded under Approaches tried so nobody retries them.

What is now rigorously proved (new this round): Lemmas 1–4 — the full reflection dictionary with side-of-line statements and non-degeneracy, the Apollonius reduction, the parallelogram law, and the directed-length power computation giving the unconditional equivalence OM = ON ⟺ (I). Proposed as a promotable lemma package. Build report at `/tmp/round-1/proof-builder-midpoint-reflection-isogonal.md` (Spec concerns: none). Per the brief, I did not fall back to the trig grind.

Proof written to results/imo-2026-02/approaches/complex-certificate.md (Status: solved)

Remaining gaps: none.

Summary of the build:
- Followed the reviewer's mandated re-encoding: dropped Im(·)=0 entirely; all interiority hypotheses are consumed in Part 0 (unsigned angles of genuine triangles, every sine positive), which is exactly what eliminates the mirror-branch obstruction.
- Eliminating χ and ψ collapses each side to a single relation linear in (b,c): ℓ_K := b sin²(φ+u) − c[cos A − cos(φ+u)cos(A+φ−u)] = 0 and its b↔c, u→v mirror ℓ_L = 0.
- The reviewer's required lemma "A, K, L not collinear" is proved synthetically (cot-monotonicity at B and C; the strict inequalities ∠ABL > ∠ABK, ∠ACK > ∠ACL force AL > AK and AK > AL on a common ray).
- Load-bearing step closed as an unconditional identity in all six free variables: 2 sinA·G = α·ℓ_K + β·ℓ_L with compact cofactors α = −[b(sin²φ+sin²v) + c sin(φ+v)sin(A−φ−v)], β = c(sin²φ+sin²u) + b sin(φ+u)sin(A−φ−u); verified in the write-up by displayed Fourier-mode cancellation tables (Id-b², Id-bc, with Id-c² by symmetry) — fully human-checkable, no "sympy verified" hand-waving.
- Build report at /tmp/round-1/proof-builder-complex-certificate.md (Spec concerns: none; includes a convergence note — this approach and secant-trig-identity now share the same core identity, so prune one if both are approved).

Proof written to results/imo-2026-02/approaches/secant-trig-identity.md (Status: solved)

Remaining gaps: none.

Summary of the build:
- **Both outline gaps closed.** The orientation/directed-length gap was eliminated structurally (chord equations X cosθ + Y sinθ = |AP| for the circle through the origin A replace tangent–chord/power arguments entirely); all configuration content is proven in Step 0 (interiority lemmas 0.1–0.3 giving the angle splittings ∠ACK = φ+χ, ∠ABL = φ+ψ and positivity of every sine used).
- **The load-bearing identity is proved.** After a division-free determinant elimination of χ, ψ, the hypotheses become the constraint pair c·N(s) = b sin²s, b·N(t) = c sin²t (s = φ+α, t = φ+β, μ = 2φ+A), and the goal reduces to the Key Identity N(s)U + N(t)V + W = sin(s−t)[N(s)N(t) − sin²s sin²t], valid on all of ℝ⁴ — proven by a frequency-bound + four-point interpolation argument (evaluations at s = t, 0, φ, μ−φ, each a few lines of product-to-sum), Factor Theorem, and continuity. The constraints make the right side vanish, giving OM = ON.
- Every displayed equation machine-verified (sympy exact for KI and the grouped computations; 33 numeric checks × 3 configurations end-to-end: `/tmp/round-1/scratch/builder_endtoend.py`, `builder_identity4.py`, `builder_final_check.py`); the prose stands alone.
- Build report: `/tmp/round-1/proof-builder-secant-trig-identity.md` (Spec concerns: none). Three promotable lemmas proposed (KI, the constraint pair, the interiority package) — KI + constraints is exactly the certificate the complex-certificate sibling seeks.

details.text: Two approaches claim complete proofs. Dispatching the proof-reviewer to adversarially judge all three builds.

details.final_text:
Review written to `/tmp/round-1/proof-reviewer.md`.

**Per-slug verdicts:**

1. **`secant-trig-identity` — APPROVE (Status: solved).** I independently verified, symbolically (sympy exp-rewrite, not just numerics): the Key Identity KI, Lemmas 4.1–4.4, all four interpolation evaluations and every displayed intermediate, and the Step-5 coefficient collection (4). I re-derived the Step-3 determinant elimination by hand, audited the interiority/angle bookkeeping (Lemmas 0.1–0.3, consequences (a)–(d)) line by line, checked the frequency-count/interpolation/density logic of Step 6, and validated the hypothesis→constraint encoding via an end-to-end configuration test I built from scratch (5 triangles incl. isoceles and obtuse; all five hypotheses verified independently; (K), (L), OM−ON all ≈ 1e-15). No gap found.

2. **`complex-certificate` — APPROVE (Status: solved).** Certificate identity (10), the bracket identity, (Id-b²)/(Id-c²)/(Id-bc), and **every individual Fourier-table row** (br1–br5, T1–T5, S1–S6, plus both decompositions) verified symbolically — all exactly as displayed. Part-3 branch-pinned elimination is sound (divisor r·sinφ > 0, valid even when sin(φ−u)=0); Part-4 non-collinearity of A, K, L is proven from the hypotheses (checked). Note: sympy's default `simplify` falsely fails on (10) — exp-rewrite closes it; numeric residuals 1e-31 confirm. This fully self-contained proof is the canonical one copied into `current.md`.

3. **`midpoint-reflection-isogonal` — CHANGES REQUESTED (Status: partial, as claimed).** Lemmas 1–4 (reflection dictionary, median/parallelogram reduction, directed-power step, identity (I)) re-checked by hand and numerically — correct, incl. the tangent-at-A case. The Step-5 gap (synthetic derivation of (I)) remains open; honest accounting, no overclaim. Recommend freezing/pruning since the problem is solved.

**Artifacts:** `results/imo-2026-02/current.md` created (Status **solved**, full proof); five lemmas certified into `results/imo-2026-02/lemmas/` (`median-reduction.md`, `AKL-noncollinear.md`, `side-relations.md`, `certificate-identity.md`, `reflection-reduction.md`); outcomes recorded (verified-milestone ×2, partial ×1); verification scripts in `/tmp/round-1/review/`.

**Overall Status: solved** — imo-2026-02 is solved with two independent, fully verified proofs.

input_data.prompt:
You are the math-explorer for problem imo-2026-02 in repo /home/agentuser/repo. Round 1, lens: **power-of-a-point / radical-axis framing**.

Problem statement (from problems.jsonl, id imo-2026-02): Let ABC be a triangle and let M and N be the midpoints of sides AB and AC. Let K and L be chosen inside triangles BMC and BNC respectively, such that K lies inside angle LBA, L lies inside angle ACK, and ∠KBA = ∠ACL, ∠LBK = ∠LNC, ∠LCK = ∠BMK. Let O be the circumcentre of triangle AKL. Prove that OM = ON.

Read `knowledge_base.md` (geometry sections) and `crux_moves_documentation.md` (then query the crux corpus, filtering domain=geometry if present — check what domains exist). The workspace `results/imo-2026-02/` is empty (round 1, no prior approaches).

Your lens: the conclusion OM = ON is equivalent to pow(M, ω) = pow(N, ω) where ω is the circumcircle of AKL (since pow(P) = OP² − R²). Since M lies on line AB through A ∈ ω and N on line AC through A ∈ ω, pow(M) = ±MA·MX where X is the second intersection of AB with ω, similarly pow(N) = ±NA·NY. Scout this terrain: what would the angle conditions need to yield? Does it reduce to showing BX = something, or AX·AB related to AY·AC, or B and C having related powers? Consider also: OM=ON ⟺ O lies on perpendicular bisector of MN, which is parallel to the perpendicular bisector of BC (MN ∥ BC, midline). Map the distinct openings within this framing, what lemmas would be needed, and which look tractable given the three angle conditions. Do NOT attempt the full proof.

Write your report to `/tmp/round-1/math-explorer-power.md`: distinct openings, needed lemmas, promising techniques, risks/dead-end warnings.
Your final message: 3-5 sentence summary of the most promising openings.
input_data.description: Explore power-of-point route

input_data.prompt:
You are the math-explorer for problem imo-2026-02 in repo /home/agentuser/repo. Round 1, lens: **spiral similarity / similar triangles / isogonality framing**.

Problem statement (from problems.jsonl, id imo-2026-02): Let ABC be a triangle and let M and N be the midpoints of sides AB and AC. Let K and L be chosen inside triangles BMC and BNC respectively, such that K lies inside angle LBA, L lies inside angle ACK, and ∠KBA = ∠ACL, ∠LBK = ∠LNC, ∠LCK = ∠BMK. Let O be the circumcentre of triangle AKL. Prove that OM = ON.

Read `knowledge_base.md` (geometry sections) and `crux_moves_documentation.md` (then query the crux corpus, filtering by domain — check what domains exist). The workspace `results/imo-2026-02/` is empty (round 1, no prior approaches).

Your lens: the three angle conditions are strikingly structured. ∠KBA = ∠ACL is a symmetric "swap" condition between (B,K) and (C,L). ∠LBK = ∠LNC ties L,B,K to the midpoint N; ∠LCK = ∠BMK ties L,C,K to midpoint M. Note ∠KBA + ∠LBK = ∠LBA and ∠ACL + ∠LCK = ∠ACK. Scout: hidden similar triangles (e.g. triangle BMK similar to some triangle, triangle CNL similar to another), spiral similarities carrying B→C or K→L, whether BKLC or MKLN might be cyclic, whether K and L are isogonal conjugates in some triangle, whether reflecting/mapping the configuration across the perpendicular bisector of BC or of MN reveals symmetry, and what role circumcircles of BMK, CNL, BLN, CKM... could play (the conditions ∠LBK=∠LNC and ∠LCK=∠BMK look like inscribed-angle conditions: ∠LBK=∠LNC suggests B,N on a circle through L? — check tangency/cyclic interpretations carefully). Map the distinct openings, the key structural conjectures worth testing, and risks. Do NOT attempt the full proof.

Write your report to `/tmp/round-1/math-explorer-similarity.md`: distinct openings, structural conjectures, needed lemmas, promising techniques, risks.
Your final message: 3-5 sentence summary of the most promising openings.
input_data.description: Explore similarity/angle route

# Olympiad Knowledge Base

Theorems and techniques for hard (difficulty-8) olympiad problems across algebra,
number theory, combinatorics, geometry, and linear algebra. Organized by technique,
not by problem domain — many problems mix areas.

## Algebra & Polynomials

- **Vieta's formulas**: roots ↔ coefficients. For systems of symmetric equations,
  treat unknowns as roots of a polynomial and read off symmetric functions.
- **Symmetric functions & Newton's identities**: convert between power sums
  `p_k = Σ xᵢᵏ` and elementary symmetric polynomials. Use to match power-sum equalities.
- **Resultants / "transform the roots"**: to show `P(r)=0 ⟹ Q(f(r))=0`, exhibit a
  polynomial identity `g(x)·P(x) = Q(f(x))`; then `P(r)=0` forces `Q(f(r))=0`.
- **Minimal-polynomial reduction**: given `P(x)=0`, reduce any high power `xᵏ` by
  repeatedly substituting the relation (e.g. `x³ = 8 − 4x`) until degree `< deg P`.
- **Sum of squares (SOS) / completing the square**: prove a real inequality by
  rewriting `LHS − RHS` as a sum of squares; equivalently a positive-semidefinite
  quadratic form.
- **Piecewise-concavity smoothing**: to minimize a function `g(φ)` that is a finite
  sum of terms each of the form `|single sinusoid|` (i.e. `|A cos(kφ)+B sin(kφ)|`),
  note each such term is concave on every interval between two consecutive zeros of
  its argument (an identically-zero term is constant, hence concave). A finite sum of
  functions concave on a common subinterval is concave there. Partitioning the circle
  by the (finitely many) zeros of all arguments gives a piecewise-concave `g`; a
  concave function on a closed interval attains its min at an endpoint, so the global
  min of `g` is at a breakpoint, where some sinusoid argument vanishes. If the term is
  a product `|x_φ · y|`, vanishing forces a factor to vanish — a "zero entry."
  Moreover, if `φ=0` is already a global minimizer and lies in the interior of a
  concavity interval, concavity forces `g` constant on that interval, so its endpoints
  are also global minimizers carrying an extra zero entry. Used to drive a minimizer to
  a sparse (axial) configuration without raising the objective.
- **Standard inequalities**: AM-GM, Cauchy-Schwarz, QM-AM, Schur. Equality cases
  pin down the extremal configuration.
- **Functional equations**: test special values, check injectivity/surjectivity.
- **Complex roots & moduli**: `|root product| = |constant term|`; conjugate
  symmetry (`b = ā`) and `aⁿ = bⁿ` cases for modulus conditions.

## Linear Algebra

- **Quadratic forms**: a symmetric form is PSD iff it's a sum of squares iff all
  eigenvalues ≥ 0. `det = 0` ⟺ nontrivial kernel.
- **Symmetric matrices**: real symmetric ⟹ orthogonally diagonalizable, real
  eigenvalues. `A²` is PSD; `Σ Aᵢ²` singular ⟺ the `Aᵢ` share a common null vector.
- **Rank / image / kernel**: `det(Σ Aᵢ Bᵢ) = 0 ∀Bᵢ` ⟺ the `Aᵢ` have a common
  nonzero vector in `∩ ker` (or images miss a direction) — argue via shared kernel.
- **Extrem...

# Crux corpus — files, schema & subtopics

The crux corpus is a retrieval bank of the load-bearing moves of solved pre-2026
Olympiad problems (disjoint from the 2026 test set), stored as two JSON arrays
joined by `problem_id`:

- `past_crux_moves_database.json` — the load-bearing move of each solved pre-2026
  problem. **2434 cruxes** across three domains: number_theory (548),
  combinatorics (1114), algebra (772).
- `past_problems_database.json` — the full statement + solution(s) of those
  problems (1026 problems; covers every `problem_id` referenced by a crux).

**Use the exact field names below. Do not guess** — `crux_move` and `statement` do
**not** exist and querying them returns blank content.

## `past_crux_moves_database.json` fields

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `problem_id` | join key |
| `technique` | the crux move — the name of the load-bearing idea |
| `how_used` | how that move cracked the problem |
| `domain` | one of `number_theory`, `combinatorics`, `algebra`; use this to filter first |
| `subtopic` | one subtopic (see list below); use this to filter within a domain |

input_data.prompt:
You are the math-explorer for problem imo-2026-02 in repo /home/agentuser/repo. Round 1, lens: **computational/numeric probing** (python with numpy/scipy/sympy available).

Problem statement (from problems.jsonl, id imo-2026-02): Let ABC be a triangle and let M and N be the midpoints of sides AB and AC. Let K and L be chosen inside triangles BMC and BNC respectively, such that K lies inside angle LBA, L lies inside angle ACK, and ∠KBA = ∠ACL, ∠LBK = ∠LNC, ∠LCK = ∠BMK. Let O be the circumcentre of triangle AKL. Prove that OM = ON.

Your job: build numeric instances of the configuration and probe structure. K and L together have 4 degrees of freedom; the 3 angle conditions leave a 1-parameter family (for generic ABC). Write python (scratch scripts under /tmp/round-1/) to: (1) fix a scalene triangle ABC, parametrize e.g. by the common angle φ = ∠KBA = ∠ACL plus another parameter, and numerically solve the constraint system (scipy fsolve on the angle conditions, taking care with signed/directed angles and the interior-position constraints); (2) verify OM = ON numerically on several instances (sanity check of the problem); (3) then TEST STRUCTURAL CONJECTURES on the solved instances, e.g.: is BKLC cyclic? is MKLN cyclic? does circle(AKL) pass through any notable point? is MK = NL or MK/NL constant? are triangles BMK and CNL similar? does line KL pass through a fixed point / stay parallel to something as the parameter varies? is the midpoint of KL notable? do circles (BMK) and (CNL) have equal radii or a common point? is A on the radical axis of some pair? does pow(M, circle AKL) = pow(N, circle AKL) split into a nicer identity like MA·MX = NA·NY with X,Y second intersections of AB, AC? Also check: as the parameter varies, does O move along a line (it should be the perpendicular bisector of MN if OM=ON always)?

Report every conjecture tested with verdict (holds to ~1e-9 / fails, with sample values). These facts are gold for the outliner. Also read `knowledge_base.md` geometry section briefly for available tools.

Write your report to `/tmp/round-1/math-explorer-numeric.md`. Final message: 3-6 sentence summary of which conjectures hold numerically.
input_data.description: Numeric probe of configuration

file.content:
## Geometry (synthetic & analytic)

- **Synthetic toolkit**: angle chasing, power of a point (and its concyclicity
  converse `PA·PB=PC·PD`), radical axes & radical center, similar triangles, trig
  cevians (Ceva/Menelaus), inversion, spiral similarity, projective ideas.
- **Circle/triangle configuration facts**: Ptolemy (`AC·BD=AB·CD+AD·BC`, with the
  `≤` inequality for any four points); Brahmagupta area; Simson line (feet from
  `P` collinear iff `P` on circumcircle); Miquel point of a complete quadrilateral;
  incircle/excircle tangency (equal tangents, touch-point reflection, homothety
  at a tangency point mapping incircle ↔ excircle).
- **Coordinates / complex / barycentric**: place coordinates to exploit symmetry;
  rotate axes to align with a key line (e.g. a tangent line `x+y=0`).
- **Conics**: a parabola tangent to a line at its vertex is `(perp. distance)² ∝
  (distance along axis)`; work in rotated coordinates.
- **Lattice-point counting**: count integer points under linear constraints
  (`x+y < c`, `x,y > 0`); combine with the algebraic/NT condition on the curve.
- **Trig identities & interval intersection**: `|x − u| ≤ v` defines an interval;
  two such hold simultaneously iff the intervals overlap (reduce to one inequality).

## Combinatorial Geometry / Incidences

import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import fsolve

def angle(P,Q,R):
    # angle at Q between P and R
    v1 = np.array(P)-np.array(Q)
    v2 = np.array(R)-np.array(Q)
    c = np.dot(v1,v2)/np.linalg.norm(v1)/np.linalg.norm(v2)
    c = np.clip(c,-1,1)
    return np.arccos(c)

# triangle
A = np.array([0.0,3.0])
B = np.array([-2.0,0.0])
C = np.array([3.0,0.0])
M = (A+B)/2
N = (A+C)/2

def equations(vars, t):
    Kx,Ky,Lx,Ly = vars
    K = np.array([Kx,Ky])
    L = np.array([Lx,Ly])

    eq2 = angle(L,B,K) - angle(L,N,C)   # angle LBK = angle LNC
    eq3 = angle(L,C,K) - angle(B,M,K)   # angle LCK = angle BMK
    eq4 = angle(A,B,K) - t              # parametrize by angle KBA = t
    return [eq1,eq2,eq3,eq4]

import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import fsolve

def angle(P,Q,R):
    v1 = np.array(P)-np.array(Q)
    v2 = np.array(R)-np.array(Q)
    c = np.dot(v1,v2)/np.linalg.norm(v1)/np.linalg.norm(v2)
    c = np.clip(c,-1,1)
    return np.arccos(c)

def circumcenter(P1,P2,P3):

def line_circle_second_intersection(P0, dirvec, O, R, exclude):
    # P0 + s*dirvec on circle, exclude is a known point (s corresponding to exclude)

    pts = [P0+s1*d, P0+s2*d]
    # pick the one not close to exclude
    if np.linalg.norm(pts[0]-exclude) < np.linalg.norm(pts[1]-exclude):
        return pts[1]
    else:

## imo-2026-02 (lens: power-of-a-point / radical-axis framing)

### Distinct openings

**Opening 1 (clean, VERIFIED, likely the right first reduction).**
For *any* point O and any segment AB with midpoint M, the "median formula" for the
quadratic function `q(P) = |P-O|^2` gives, purely by the parallelogram law (no circle
needed):
`q(M) = (q(A)+q(B))/2 - AB^2/4`.
Apply this to O = circumcenter of AKL, to (A,B,M) and to (A,C,N):
`OM^2 = (OA^2+OB^2)/2 - AB^2/4`, `ON^2 = (OA^2+OC^2)/2 - AC^2/4`.
Subtracting, OA^2 cancels, and:
`OM = ON  ⟺  OB^2 - OC^2 = (AB^2 - AC^2)/2 = (c^2-b^2)/2` (standard notation b=CA,c=AB).
I verified this identity is exactly equivalent to OM=ON by direct coordinate computation
(not just conjectured — it is elementary vector algebra, true unconditionally).
**Important nuance found by testing:** this step by itself does *not* yet use that O is
the circumcenter of AKL — it is true for any point O. The real content of the problem is
showing this specific O (built from K, L via the three angle conditions) satisfies
`OB^2-OC^2=(c^2-b^2)/2`. So this is a valid, clean *reformulation* of the goal, but not
a proof by itself; the outliner should not mistake reaching "OB²-OC²=(c²-b²)/2" for a
free step — it still requires the angle-condition machinery.

**Opening 2 (power-of-a-point via chords through B, C).**
Since K, L, A all lie on ω = circumcircle(AKL): pow(B,ω) = BO²-R² can be computed as a
signed product along line BK: pow(B,ω) = `\overline{BK}·\overline{BK'}` where K' is the
second intersection of line BK with ω (similarly pow(C,ω) = `\overline{CL}·\overline{CL'}`
via line CL). Combined with pow(A,ω)=0, Opening 1's identity becomes
pow(B,ω) - pow(C,ω) = (c²-b²)/2, i.e. `BK·BK' - CL·CL' = (c²-b²)/2` (signed). This is
the natural place to plug in the angle conditions, since BK and CL are exactly the
segments constrained by ∠KBA = ∠ACL (condition 1). **I checked numerically whether K'
(second intersection of BK with ω) coincides with a natural named point (L, N, or C)
— it does not**, in a generic scalene example; ruling out that naive guess. The second
intersection points do not have an obvious closed form from the given data alone, so
this chord-product route likely needs an auxiliary construction (e.g. locating K', L'
via an explicit angle-chase using the inscribed-angle theorem in ω, or avoiding K'
entirely and instead computing pow(B,ω) via the extended law of sines: for P off a
circle of radius R centered O, and a chord through P and X∈ω making angle φ with the
tangent at X, pow(P)=PX·PX' with PX' derivable from the inscribed angle — needs a
concrete angle-chase to be useful).

ALWAYS: for a hard geometry problem with a free/underdetermined configuration (fewer equations than coordinates for the constructed points), use scipy.optimize.fsolve with several random initial guesses plus explicit region-validity filters (point-in-triangle, angle-ordering checks) to find genuinely valid instances before trusting any numeric relation — spurious/wrong-branch solutions that violate the stated inequalities (e.g. "K inside angle LBA") can satisfy the equalities but give false-looking counterexamples (round 1, imo-2026-02: unfiltered fsolve solutions broke OM=ON, filtered ones did not).
ALWAYS: when a "prove OM=ON" (equidistance from two points) goal involves a circumcenter O of a triangle containing a fixed vertex shared with both target points' construction (here A, with M,N midpoints of AB,AC), test the power-of-a-point reformulation pow(M,ω)=pow(N,ω) via the secant through A — it is numerically cheap to verify and often a sharper synthetic target than computing O directly (round 1, imo-2026-02).
NEVER: assume the crux corpus has geometry entries — crux_moves_documentation.md states geometry cruxes have not been extracted yet (only number_theory/combinatorics/algebra); check this once and skip further corpus queries for geometry problems (round 1, imo-2026-02).

## imo-2026-02

### Setup used for numerics
Coordinates: A=(0,0), B=(5,0), C=(1.3,4.1) (triangle 1, scalene) and a second, very
different triangle A=(0,0), B=(7,1), C=(-1,5.5) (triangle 2) for cross-checking.
M,N = midpoints of AB, AC. Parametrized the 1-parameter family by φ = ∠KBA = ∠ACL:
K lies on the ray from B obtained by rotating BA by φ toward the interior (chosen by
picking the rotation sign that points toward C); similarly L on the ray from C
rotating CA by φ toward B. For fixed φ, the remaining two conditions
∠LBK = ∠LNC (angle at N, between NL and NC — **note: NOT the angle at L**, a bug I
had to fix) and ∠LCK = ∠BMK (angle at M, between MB and MK) give 2 equations in the
2 remaining free parameters (distances of K, L along their rays); solved by
`scipy.optimize.fsolve` from a grid of initial guesses, keeping only solutions with
K strictly inside △BMC, L strictly inside △BNC, K inside ∠LBA, and L inside ∠ACK
(checked via signed angles). Scripts: `/tmp/round-1/scratch/probe3.py` (triangle 1,
final working solver), `/tmp/round-1/scratch/probe9.py` (triangle 2, cross-check),
`probe4.py`–`probe8.py` (conjecture tests).

For triangle 1 the valid range is roughly φ ∈ (0°, ~48°) (fsolve/interior checks
fail outside; at φ=50° no valid interior solution was found in the search grid —
plausibly the family's endpoint, not investigated further).

### Main result: OM = ON — CONFIRMED NUMERICALLY (strong evidence)
On triangle 1, φ = 10°,15°,...,45° (8 points), all interior conditions verified
True, and OM = ON to machine precision (|OM−ON| ≤ 2e-15 in every case, e.g.
φ=20°: OM=ON=1.3818899425). On triangle 2 (different shape), φ=5°..30°, same:
diff ≤ 2e-15 in all 6 samples. This is strong numeric confirmation the statement
is correctly transcribed and true; treat as **conjecture verified numerically**,
not a proof.

### Stronger fact than OM=ON: O lies exactly on the perpendicular bisector of MN
Directly checked (O − midpoint(MN))·(N−M) ≈ 1e-15 for every sample on both
triangles. This is the same fact as OM=ON restated (a point is equidistant from M,N
iff it's on the perp bisector), but it's worth flagging to the outliner as the
natural reformulation: **prove O lies on line ⊥ MN through its midpoint**, e.g. by
locating O via two independent characterizations (equal power, or an explicit
second point construction) rather than computing two distances.

### Conjectures tested and their verdicts (triangle 1, several φ)
- **BKLC concyclic** — FALSE. Normalized power of K w.r.t. circumcircle(B,L,C) is
  nonzero and shrinks toward 0 only as φ approaches the family's boundary (~45°+),
  e.g. φ=10°: pow/R²=−0.155; φ=45°: pow/R²=−0.024. Not exactly 0 anywhere in the
  interior tested. Verdict: **fails**.
- **MKLN concyclic** — FALSE similarly (pow(K,MLN)/R² = 0.027 at φ=10° down to
  0.0015 at φ=45°, never exactly 0). Verdict: **fails**.
- **MK = NL** (equal segments) — FALSE but close: MK/NL ranges 0.945 (φ=10°) up to
  0.999 (φ=45°), approaching but not rea...

file.content:
## imo-2026-02 (lens: power-of-a-point / radical-axis framing)

### Distinct openings

**Opening 1 (clean, VERIFIED, likely the right first reduction).**
For *any* point O and any segment AB with midpoint M, the "median formula" for the
quadratic function `q(P) = |P-O|^2` gives, purely by the parallelogram law (no circle
needed):
`q(M) = (q(A)+q(B))/2 - AB^2/4`.
Apply this to O = circumcenter of AKL, to (A,B,M) and to (A,C,N):
`OM^2 = (OA^2+OB^2)/2 - AB^2/4`, `ON^2 = (OA^2+OC^2)/2 - AC^2/4`.
Subtracting, OA^2 cancels, and:
`OM = ON  ⟺  OB^2 - OC^2 = (AB^2 - AC^2)/2 = (c^2-b^2)/2` (standard notation b=CA,c=AB).
I verified this identity is exactly equivalent to OM=ON by direct coordinate computation
(not just conjectured — it is elementary vector algebra, true unconditionally).
**Important nuance found by testing:** this step by itself does *not* yet use that O is
the circumcenter of AKL — it is true for any point O. The real content of the problem is
showing this specific O (built from K, L via the three angle conditions) satisfies
`OB^2-OC^2=(c^2-b^2)/2`. So this is a valid, clean *reformulation* of the goal, but not
a proof by itself; the outliner should not mistake reaching "OB²-OC²=(c²-b²)/2" for a
free step — it still requires the angle-condition machinery.

**Opening 2 (power-of-a-point via chords through B, C).**
Since K, L, A all lie on ω = circumcircle(AKL): pow(B,ω) = BO²-R² can be computed as a
signed product along line BK: pow(B,ω) = `\overline{BK}·\overline{BK'}` where K' is the
second intersection of line BK with ω (similarly pow(C,ω) = `\overline{CL}·\overline{CL'}`
via line CL). Combined with pow(A,ω)=0, Opening 1's identity becomes
pow(B,ω) - pow(C,ω) = (c²-b²)/2, i.e. `BK·BK' - CL·CL' = (c²-b²)/2` (signed). This is
the natural place to plug in the angle conditions, since BK and CL are exactly the
segments constrained by ∠KBA = ∠ACL (condition 1). **I checked numerically whether K'
(second intersection of BK with ω) coincides with a natural named point (L, N, or C)
— it does not**, in a generic scalene example; ruling out that naive guess. The second
intersection points do not have an obvious closed form from the given data alone, so
this chord-product route likely needs an auxiliary construction (e.g. locating K', L'
via an explicit angle-chase using the inscribed-angle theorem in ω, or avoiding K'
entirely and instead computing pow(B,ω) via the extended law of sines: for P off a
circle of radius R centered O, and a chord through P and X∈ω making angle φ with the
tangent at X, pow(P)=PX·PX' with PX' derivable from the inscribed angle — needs a
concrete angle-chase to be useful).

**Opening 3 (angle conditions as PARTIAL spiral similarities — checked and only partly
true, an important caution).** The shapes of conditions 2 and 3 (∠LBK=∠LNC, ∠LCK=∠BMK)
look like halves of the "spiral similarity center" configuration (L centered, sending
B→N,K→C; K centered, sending C→M,L→B). I tested this numerically: the *angle* equality
holds (by...

## imo-2026-02

- Distinct openings:
  1. **Power-of-a-point reformulation (strongest lead).** OM=ON is equivalent to
     pow(M, ⊙AKL) = pow(N, ⊙AKL). Since A ∈ ⊙AKL and M is the midpoint of AB, the
     power of M along line AB is the signed product MA·MP where P is the *second*
     intersection of line AB with ⊙AKL; similarly pow(N,⊙AKL) = NA·NQ with Q the
     second intersection of line AC with ⊙AKL. Numerically confirmed MA·MP = NA·NQ
     to ~1e-9 in an independent scalene example. This turns the problem into: show
     these two directed-length products along AB, AC are equal — a route that avoids
     ever locating O explicitly and only needs the two secant lengths AP, AQ (or
     equivalently the chord lengths KP-type relations via power of B and C too,
     since B, C are *not* generally on the circle). This looks like the most
     promising synthetic target for the outliner: identify P (second meet of AB with
     ⊙AKL) and Q (second meet of AC with ⊙AKL) synthetically (likely via the angle
     conditions turning into inscribed-angle statements ∠AKP-type or ∠ALQ-type) and
     show AM·AP-type / AN·AQ-type quantities agree — probably by proving AP/AQ has a

## imo-2026-02

### Setup used for numerics
Coordinates: A=(0,0), B=(5,0), C=(1.3,4.1) (triangle 1, scalene) and a second, very
different triangle A=(0,0), B=(7,1), C=(-1,5.5) (triangle 2) for cross-checking.
M,N = midpoints of AB, AC. Parametrized the 1-parameter family by φ = ∠KBA = ∠ACL:
K lies on the ray from B obtained by rotating BA by φ toward the interior (chosen by
picking the rotation sign that points toward C); similarly L on the ray from C
rotating CA by φ toward B. For fixed φ, the remaining two conditions
∠LBK = ∠LNC (angle at N, between NL and NC — **note: NOT the angle at L**, a bug I
had to fix) and ∠LCK = ∠BMK (angle at M, between MB and MK) give 2 equations in the
2 remaining free parameters (distances of K, L along their rays); solved by
`scipy.optimize.fsolve` from a grid of initial guesses, keeping only solutions with
K strictly inside △BMC, L strictly inside △BNC, K inside ∠LBA, and L inside ∠ACK
(checked via signed angles). Scripts: `/tmp/round-1/scratch/probe3.py` (triangle 1,
final working solver), `/tmp/round-1/scratch/probe9.py` (triangle 2, cross-check),
`probe4.py`–`probe8.py` (conjecture tests).

For triangle 1 the valid range is roughly φ ∈ (0°, ~48°) (fsolve/interior checks
fail outside; at φ=50° no valid interior solution was found in the search grid —
plausibly the family's endpoint, not investigated further).

### Main result: OM = ON — CONFIRMED NUMERICALLY (strong evidence)
On triangle 1, φ = 10°,15°,...,45° (8 points), all interior conditions verified
True, and OM = ON to machine precision (|OM−ON| ≤ 2e-15 in every case, e.g.
φ=20°: OM=ON=1.3818899425). On triangle 2 (different shape), φ=5°..30°, same:
diff ≤ 2e-15 in all 6 samples. This is strong numeric confirmation the statement
is correctly transcribed and true; treat as **conjecture verified numerically**,
not a proof.

### Stronger fact than OM=ON: O lies exactly on the perpendicular bisector of MN
Directly checked (O − midpoint(MN))·(N−M) ≈ 1e-15 for every sample on both
triangles. This is the same fact as OM=ON restated (a point is equidistant from M,N
iff it's on the perp bisector), but it's worth flagging to the outliner as the
natural reformulation: **prove O lies on line ⊥ MN through its midpoint**, e.g. by
locating O via two independent characterizations (equal power, or an explicit
second point construction) rather than computing two distances.

### Conjectures tested and their verdicts (triangle 1, several φ)
- **BKLC concyclic** — FALSE. Normalized power of K w.r.t. circumcircle(B,L,C) is
  nonzero and shrinks toward 0 only as φ approaches the family's boundary (~45°+),
  e.g. φ=10°: pow/R²=−0.155; φ=45°: pow/R²=−0.024. Not exactly 0 anywhere in the
  interior tested. Verdict: **fails**.
- **MKLN concyclic** — FALSE similarly (pow(K,MLN)/R² = 0.027 at φ=10° down to
  0.0015 at φ=45°, never exactly 0). Verdict: **fails**.
- **MK = NL** (equal segments) — FALSE but close: MK/NL ranges 0.945 (φ=10°) up to
  0.999 (φ=45°), approaching but not rea...

import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import fsolve

def ang(u, v):
    cu = u/np.linalg.norm(u); cv = v/np.linalg.norm(v)
    d = np.clip(np.dot(cu,cv), -1, 1)
    return np.arccos(d)

def rot(v, theta):
    c,s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
    return np.array([[c,-s],[s,c]])@v

def signed_ang(u, v):
    a = np.arctan2(v[1],v[0]) - np.arctan2(u[1],u[0])
    while a > np.pi: a -= 2*np.pi
    while a <= -np.pi: a += 2*np.pi
    return a

## Current best
Verified reduction chain (steps 1–4 below are established or elementary); the trig
constraint system E1–E6 and the tangent-chord form of the goal were verified
numerically to machine precision (round-1 outliner probes). Nothing proved beyond
the unconditional reductions yet.

## Outline

**Target (whole problem):** OM = ON for O the circumcentre of AKL.

**Notation.** ∠A = ∠BAC, b = CA, c = AB. φ = ∠KBA = ∠ACL. α_K = ∠BAK, α_L = ∠CAL,
ψ = ∠LBK = ∠LNC, χ = ∠LCK = ∠BMK. ω = circumcircle of AKL, radius R, centre O.
All angle bookkeeping should be done with the configuration hypotheses
(K inside △BMC, L inside △BNC, K inside ∠LBA, L inside ∠ACK) fixing every sign;
these give in particular ∠ABL = φ + ψ and ∠ACK = φ + χ, and that rays BM = BA
(M on segment AB) and CN = CA (N on segment AC).

**Step 1 (reduction, unconditional).** For any point O, by the median formula
(parallelogram law applied to q(P)=|P−O|²):
OM² = (OA²+OB²)/2 − c²/4 and ON² = (OA²+OC²)/2 − b²/4. Hence
OM = ON ⟺ OB² − OC² = (c² − b²)/2.
This is elementary vector algebra, true for every point O; the content of the
problem is that THIS O satisfies it.

**Step 2 (power form).** Let P, Q be the second intersections of lines AB, AC with ω
(A ∈ ω). Then pow(B,ω) = OB² − R² = \vec{BA}·\vec{BP} = c(c − AP) and
pow(C,ω) = b(b − AQ) (directed lengths along the secants through A). So
OM = ON ⟺ c·AP − b·AQ = (c² − b²)/2.
(Numerically verified: both sides equal (c²−b²)/2 across the whole 1-parameter
family in two scalene triangles.)

**Step 3 (tangent-chord parametrization of ω at A).** Let δ be the directed angle
from the tangent of ω at A to ray AB (orientation chosen so all four chords come
out positive; verified consistent numerically). By the tangent–chord angle and the
extended law of sines in ω:
AP = 2R sin δ, AQ = 2R sin(δ + ∠A), AK = 2R sin(δ + α_K), AL = 2R sin(δ + ∠A − α_L).
The last two equations determine (R, δ) from (AK, AL, α_K, α_L, ∠A).

**Step 4 (hypotheses as a clean trig system — the midpoints enter here).**
Law of sines in the five triangles ABK, ACL, MBK, NCL, ACK, ABL gives:
- E1: AK = c sin φ / sin(φ + α_K)            (△ABK: ∠ABK = φ)
- E2: AL = b sin φ / sin(φ + α_L)            (△ACL: ∠ACL = φ)
- E3: 2 sin α_K sin(φ + χ) = sin χ sin(φ + α_K)
  — because BK computed in △ABK equals BK computed in △MBK, where ∠KBM = φ
  (M on ray BA), ∠BMK = χ, and MB = c/2; the factor 2 is exactly the midpoint.
- E4: 2 sin α_L sin(φ + ψ) = sin ψ sin(φ + α_L)   (same mechanism at N, NC = b/2)
- E5: AK = b sin(φ + χ) / sin(φ + χ + ∠A − α_K)  (△ACK: ∠ACK = φ + χ)
- E6: AL = c sin(φ + ψ) / sin(φ + ψ + ∠A − α_L)  (△ABL: ∠ABL = φ + ψ)
All six verified to ~1e-15 on the solved configurations.

**Step 5 (decoupled structure).** E1&E5&E3 involve only (α_K, χ) [plus φ, ∠A, b, c];
E2&E6&E4 involve only (α_L, ψ). Each side is 2 independent constraints on 2
unknowns (E1&E5 combine to o...

# Approach: midpoint-reflection-isogonal

## Status
unsolved

## Approaches tried
- (none yet — opened round 1)

## Current best
Steps 1–3 below (the reflection dictionary) are exact theorems (parallelogram
facts), each verified numerically to machine precision by the round-1 outliner
probes. The final identity (Step 5) is open.

## Outline

**Target (whole problem):** OM = ON for O the circumcentre ω = ⊙(AKL).

**Framing.** Kill the midpoints entirely by reflecting K in M and L in N; the
whole configuration transfers to two points on a pair of ISOGONAL rays through A,
and OM = ON becomes a power identity at the reflected points. This is a synthetic
route that never parametrizes ω.

**Step 1 (reflection dictionary — exact, by the parallelogram AKBK\*, ALCL\*).**
Let K\* = 2M − K, L\* = 2N − L. Since M is the midpoint of both AB and KK\*,
AKBK\* is a parallelogram; likewise ALCL\*. Consequently (all verified exactly):
- AK\* = BK and AK\* ∥ KB; AL\* = CL and AL\* ∥ LC;
- ∠K\*AB = ∠KBA = φ and ∠L\*AC = ∠ACL = φ — so K\* and L\* lie on the two rays
  from A making angle φ with AB and with AC respectively: an isogonal pair in ∠A;
- ∠AMK\* = ∠BMK (vertical angles at M) = ∠LCK, and ∠ANL\* = ∠LNC = ∠LBK.
So the three hypotheses become: K\*, L\* on isogonal rays r₁, r₂ through A, with

OM = ON ⟺ pow(K\*, ω) − KK\*²/2 = pow(L\*, ω) − LL\*²/2.

**Step 3 (parallelogram law).** KK\*² = 2AK² + 2BK² − c² (diagonals of AKBK\*),
LL\*² = 2AL² + 2CL² − b². Substituting and writing pow(K\*,ω) = AK\*² − AK\*·AP₁
= BK² − BK·AP₁ (secant from K\* through A ∈ ω; P₁ = second meet of ray r₁ with ω),
and likewise pow(L\*,ω) = CL² − CL·AP₂ (P₂ = second meet of r₂ with ω), the goal
becomes:

**Step 4 (what the isogonality buys).** P₁, P₂ are the second intersections of ω
with an isogonal pair of rays at A ∈ ω. By the inscribed-angle theorem the chords
AP₁ = 2R sin∠(r₁, tangent at A), AP₂ = 2R sin∠(r₂, tangent at A), and the
isogonality means ∠(r₁, AB) = ∠(r₂, AC) = φ, i.e. the four directions AB, r₁, r₂,
AC are symmetric in pairs about the ∠A-bisector. Also AK·AP₁-type products:
pow of any point of r₁ against ω is computable along r₁. Key structural hope:
combine the chord AK (also from A) with AP₁ via ∠KAP₁ = |∠A − α_K − φ|-type
relations so that BK·AP₁ + AK² collapses to c·(something) — mirroring the
secant identity c·AP − b·AQ = (c²−b²)/2 of the sibling approach but along the
isogonal rays.

∠AMK\* = ∠LCK and ∠ANL\* = ∠LBK into a synthetic statement about P₁, P₂ (e.g. a
concyclicity involving K\*, L\*, M, N or a spiral similarity centred at A carrying
(K\*, P₁-data) to (L\*, P₂-data)), st...

# Approach: complex-certificate

## Status
unsolved

## Approaches tried
- (none yet — opened round 1)

## Current best
Nothing established beyond the unconditional reduction OM = ON ⟺
OB² − OC² = (AB² − AC²)/2 (shared with the other approaches; elementary
median-formula vector algebra).

## Outline

**Target (whole problem):** OM = ON for O the circumcentre of AKL.

**Framing.** Pure algebra with an explicit polynomial certificate: encode the two
"linking" angle conditions as real-vanishing rational expressions, encode the goal
the same way, and exhibit the goal as an explicit algebraic combination of the
hypotheses. Machine-assisted discovery (sympy resultants / Groebner) is allowed;
the deliverable is a human-checkable identity chain in prose.

**Step 1 (parametrize away condition 1).** Work in ℂ, A = 0. Condition
∠KBA = φ says K lies on the ray from B obtained by rotating ray BA by ±φ; the
configuration hypotheses fix the sign. So write
  K = B + t e^{−iφ} (A − B)/|A − B| · |A−B| = B(1 − t e^{−iφ}),  t ∈ (0, 1)-scale,
  L = C (1 − s e^{+iφ}),
(after normalizing directions; the two opposite rotation signs are forced by
"K on the C-side of AB", "L on the B-side of AC"). This uses up condition 1
exactly and leaves real unknowns t, s > 0 and φ.

**Step 2 (conditions 2 and 3 as real-part equations).** With M = B/2, N = C/2:
∠LBK = ∠LNC becomes
  Im [ ((K−B)/(L−B)) · conj-free ratio ] -form:  arg((K−B)/(L−B)) = arg((C−N)/(L−N)),
i.e.  H₁ := Im( (K−B)(L−N) / ((L−B)(C−N)) ) = 0 with the correct branch
(equal signed angles, not mod π — the interior hypotheses pin the branch, and the
real part of the same ratio is positive, which should be recorded as the branch
condition). Similarly
  H₂ := Im( (K−C)(B−M) / ((L−C)(K−M)) ) = 0 for ∠LCK = ∠BMK.
Clearing denominators, H₁, H₂ are real polynomial equations in
(t, s, cos φ, sin φ, Re B, Im B, Re C, Im C).

**Step 3 (goal as a polynomial).** O is the circumcentre of A=0, K, L:
O = (|K|² L − |L|² K) · i / (2 Im(conj(K) L)) — hmm, standard formula:
O = ( |K|² L − |L|² K ) / ( conj(K) L − K conj(L) ) · (appropriate conjugation);
use the standard determinant formula for the circumcentre through 0, K, L.
Goal G := |O − B/2|² − |O − C/2|² = 0. Equivalently (cleaner, avoids O entirely):
G' := pow(B, ω) − pow(C, ω) − (|B|² − |C|²)/2 = 0, where pow(P, ω) =
|P|² − Re( conj(P) (K + L) ) + Re( conj(K) L )-type expression obtained from the
circle through 0, K, L: the circle is { z : |z|² = Re( conj(w) z ) } with
w = (|K|² L̄ − ... ) — derive once, carefully. pow(P) = |P|² − Re( conj(w) P )
where w is determined linearly by |K|² = Re( conj(w) K ), |L|² = Re( conj(w) L ).
This makes G' a RATIONAL function with denominator Im( conj(K) L ) ≠ 0
(A, K, L not collinear — must be justified from the configuration).

**Step 4 (elimination / certificate).**
Show G' vanishes on the variety { H₁ = 0, H₂ = 0, branch conditions } — e.g.
compute Res_t(H₁, H₂)-style elimination, or find polynomials λ₁, λ₂ and a
nonvanishing multiplier μ ...

## Status
unsolved

## Approaches tried
- (none yet — opened round 1)

## Current best
Nothing established beyond the shared unconditional reduction
OM = ON ⟺ F := pow(B, ω) − pow(C, ω) = (c² − b²)/2, and the numerically observed
facts that (i) the valid (K, L) form a smooth 1-parameter family in φ = ∠KBA, and
(ii) F is exactly constant along it.

## Outline

**Target (whole problem):** OM = ON for O the circumcentre ω = ⊙(AKL).

**Framing (deformation, not identity-grinding).** The hypotheses cut out a
1-parameter family of configurations (φ, K(φ), L(φ)) for a fixed triangle. Prove
the target quantity F(φ) = pow(B, ω_φ) − pow(C, ω_φ) is CONSTANT along the family,
then compute its value at one distinguished (limiting/degenerate) member where the
geometry collapses to something computable. This replaces one hard global identity
by (a) a differential statement and (b) one explicit limit evaluation.

**Step 1 (reduction).** OM = ON ⟺ F = (c² − b²)/2 — median formula, unconditional.

**Step 2 (the family is a smooth curve).** Using the trig system of the sibling
approach (E1–E6 there), for each φ in the valid open interval the pair (α_K, χ) is
determined by the K-side 2×2 system and (α_L, ψ) by the L-side system; the
solutions depend real-analytically on φ by the implicit function theorem, provided
the Jacobian of each 2×2 system is nonzero on the valid range.
**Gap:** verify the Jacobian nonvanishing (or restate via a monotonicity argument:
e.g. show χ and α_K are strictly monotone in φ).

**Step 3 (invariance: F'(φ) = 0).** Differentiate F along the family. Structural
route rather than brute force: F = pow(B,ω) − pow(C,ω) is a linear functional of
the circle ω (in the (center, |center|²−R²) coordinates, pow(P, ·) is affine), so
F' depends only on the velocity of ω in the 3-dimensional space of circles. The
circle ω_φ always passes through the FIXED point A, so its velocity lies in the
2-dim space of circles through A; F' = 0 for all φ iff the velocity stays in the
1-dim subspace of directions along which pow(B) − pow(C) is unchanged — i.e. iff
the derivative circle-direction is "radical-axis-parallel to line BC-difference".
Concretely: writing circle as |z|² − 2⟨o, z⟩ + e = 0 (o = center, e = pow(0)),
F = −2⟨o, B − C⟩ + (|B|² − |C|²), so F' = −2⟨o'(φ), B − C⟩ and the whole claim is:
  **the centre O(φ) moves PERPENDICULAR to BC** — exactly the explorer's observed
"O slides along the perpendicular bisector of MN" (that line is ⊥ BC).
**Gap (load-bearing):** prove ⟨O'(φ), B − C⟩ = 0 from the differentiated
constraint system. This is one bilinear identity in the differentials of E1–E6;
it may factor more cleanly than the finite identity (constants of motion often
do). If it resists, this approach dies here — record why.

**Step 4 (anchor: evaluate F at a boundary/limit member).** As φ tends to an
endpoint of the valid interval the configuration degenerates (numerics: K, L
approach the boundaries of their triangl...

# Approach: secant-trig-identity

## Status
unsolved

## Approaches tried
- (none yet — opened round 1)

## Current best
Verified reduction chain (steps 1–4 below are established or elementary); the trig
constraint system E1–E6 and the tangent-chord form of the goal were verified
numerically to machine precision (round-1 outliner probes). Nothing proved beyond
the unconditional reductions yet.

## Outline

**Target (whole problem):** OM = ON for O the circumcentre of AKL.

**Notation.** ∠A = ∠BAC, b = CA, c = AB. φ = ∠KBA = ∠ACL. α_K = ∠BAK, α_L = ∠CAL,
ψ = ∠LBK = ∠LNC, χ = ∠LCK = ∠BMK. ω = circumcircle of AKL, radius R, centre O.
All angle bookkeeping should be done with the configuration hypotheses
(K inside △BMC, L inside △BNC, K inside ∠LBA, L inside ∠ACK) fixing every sign;
these give in particular ∠ABL = φ + ψ and ∠ACK = φ + χ, and that rays BM = BA
(M on segment AB) and CN = CA (N on segment AC).

**Step 1 (reduction, unconditional).** For any point O, by the median formula
(parallelogram law applied to q(P)=|P−O|²):
OM² = (OA²+OB²)/2 − c²/4 and ON² = (OA²+OC²)/2 − b²/4. Hence
OM = ON ⟺ OB² − OC² = (c² − b²)/2.
This is elementary vector algebra, true for every point O; the content of the
problem is that THIS O satisfies it.

**Step 2 (power form).** Let P, Q be the second intersections of lines AB, AC with ω
(A ∈ ω). Then pow(B,ω) = OB² − R² = \vec{BA}·\vec{BP} = c(c − AP) and
pow(C,ω) = b(b − AQ) (directed lengths along the secants through A). So
OM = ON ⟺ c·AP − b·AQ = (c² − b²)/2.
(Numerically verified: both sides equal (c²−b²)/2 across the whole 1-parameter
family in two scalene triangles.)

**Step 3 (tangent-chord parametrization of ω at A).** Let δ be the directed angle
from the tangent of ω at A to ray AB (orientation chosen so all four chords come
out positive; verified consistent numerically). By the tangent–chord angle and the
extended law of sines in ω:
AP = 2R sin δ, AQ = 2R sin(δ + ∠A), AK = 2R sin(δ + α_K), AL = 2R sin(δ + ∠A − α_L).
The last two equations determine (R, δ) from (AK, AL, α_K, α_L, ∠A).

**Step 4 (hypotheses as a clean trig system — the midpoints enter here).**
Law of sines in the five triangles ABK, ACL, MBK, NCL, ACK, ABL gives:
- E1: AK = c sin φ / sin(φ + α_K)            (△ABK: ∠ABK = φ)
- E2: AL = b sin φ / sin(φ + α_L)            (△ACL: ∠ACL = φ)
- E3: 2 sin α_K sin(φ + χ) = sin χ sin(φ + α_K)
  — because BK computed in △ABK equals BK computed in △MBK, where ∠KBM = φ
  (M on ray BA), ∠BMK = χ, and MB = c/2; the factor 2 is exactly the midpoint.
- E4: 2 sin α_L sin(φ + ψ) = sin ψ sin(φ + α_L)   (same mechanism at N, NC = b/2)
- E5: AK = b sin(φ + χ) / sin(φ + χ + ∠A − α_K)  (△ACK: ∠ACK = φ + χ)
- E6: AL = c sin(φ + ψ) / sin(φ + ψ + ∠A − α_L)  (△ABL: ∠ABL = φ + ψ)
All six verified to ~1e-15 on the solved configurations.

**Step 5 (decoupled structure).** E1&E5&E3 involve only (α_K, χ) [plus φ, ∠A, b, c];
E2&E6&E4 involve only (α_L, ψ). Each side is 2 independent constraints on 2
unknowns (E1&E5 combine to o...

# Approach: midpoint-reflection-isogonal

## Status
unsolved

## Approaches tried
- (none yet — opened round 1)

## Current best
Steps 1–3 below (the reflection dictionary) are exact theorems (parallelogram
facts), each verified numerically to machine precision by the round-1 outliner
probes. The final identity (Step 5) is open.

## Outline

**Target (whole problem):** OM = ON for O the circumcentre ω = ⊙(AKL).

**Framing.** Kill the midpoints entirely by reflecting K in M and L in N; the
whole configuration transfers to two points on a pair of ISOGONAL rays through A,
and OM = ON becomes a power identity at the reflected points. This is a synthetic
route that never parametrizes ω.

**Step 1 (reflection dictionary — exact, by the parallelogram AKBK\*, ALCL\*).**
Let K\* = 2M − K, L\* = 2N − L. Since M is the midpoint of both AB and KK\*,
AKBK\* is a parallelogram; likewise ALCL\*. Consequently (all verified exactly):
- AK\* = BK and AK\* ∥ KB; AL\* = CL and AL\* ∥ LC;
- ∠K\*AB = ∠KBA = φ and ∠L\*AC = ∠ACL = φ — so K\* and L\* lie on the two rays
  from A making angle φ with AB and with AC respectively: an isogonal pair in ∠A;
- ∠AMK\* = ∠BMK (vertical angles at M) = ∠LCK, and ∠ANL\* = ∠LNC = ∠LBK.
So the three hypotheses become: K\*, L\* on isogonal rays r₁, r₂ through A, with
∠AMK\* = ∠LCK and ∠ANL\* = ∠LBK, where K = 2M − K\*, L = 2N − L\*.

**Step 2 (goal in reflected form — median formula).** M is the midpoint of KK\*, so
OM² = (OK² + OK\*²)/2 − KK\*²/4 = (R² + OK\*²)/2 − KK\*²/4 (K ∈ ω). Same at N. So
OM = ON ⟺ pow(K\*, ω) − KK\*²/2 = pow(L\*, ω) − LL\*²/2.

**Step 3 (parallelogram law).** KK\*² = 2AK² + 2BK² − c² (diagonals of AKBK\*),
LL\*² = 2AL² + 2CL² − b². Substituting and writing pow(K\*,ω) = AK\*² − AK\*·AP₁
= BK² − BK·AP₁ (secant from K\* through A ∈ ω; P₁ = second meet of ray r₁ with ω),
and likewise pow(L\*,ω) = CL² − CL·AP₂ (P₂ = second meet of r₂ with ω), the goal
becomes:
  **BK·AP₁ + AK² − (CL·AP₂ + AL²) = (c² − b²)/2.**
(Consistent numerically with all probes.)

**Step 4 (what the isogonality buys).** P₁, P₂ are the second intersections of ω
with an isogonal pair of rays at A ∈ ω. By the inscribed-angle theorem the chords
AP₁ = 2R sin∠(r₁, tangent at A), AP₂ = 2R sin∠(r₂, tangent at A), and the
isogonality means ∠(r₁, AB) = ∠(r₂, AC) = φ, i.e. the four directions AB, r₁, r₂,
AC are symmetric in pairs about the ∠A-bisector. Also AK·AP₁-type products:
pow of any point of r₁ against ω is computable along r₁. Key structural hope:
combine the chord AK (also from A) with AP₁ via ∠KAP₁ = |∠A − α_K − φ|-type
relations so that BK·AP₁ + AK² collapses to c·(something) — mirroring the
secant identity c·AP − b·AQ = (c²−b²)/2 of the sibling approach but along the
isogonal rays.
**Gap (load-bearing):** turn the two transferred angle conditions
∠AMK\* = ∠LCK and ∠ANL\* = ∠LBK into a synthetic statement about P₁, P₂ (e.g. a
concyclicity involving K\*, L\*, M, N or a spiral similarity centred at A carrying
(K\*, P₁-data) to (L\*, P₂-data)), st...

# Approach: complex-certificate

## Status
unsolved

## Approaches tried
- (none yet — opened round 1)

## Current best
Nothing established beyond the unconditional reduction OM = ON ⟺
OB² − OC² = (AB² − AC²)/2 (shared with the other approaches; elementary
median-formula vector algebra).

## Outline

**Target (whole problem):** OM = ON for O the circumcentre of AKL.

**Framing.** Pure algebra with an explicit polynomial certificate: encode the two
"linking" angle conditions as real-vanishing rational expressions, encode the goal
the same way, and exhibit the goal as an explicit algebraic combination of the
hypotheses. Machine-assisted discovery (sympy resultants / Groebner) is allowed;
the deliverable is a human-checkable identity chain in prose.

**Step 1 (parametrize away condition 1).** Work in ℂ, A = 0. Condition
∠KBA = φ says K lies on the ray from B obtained by rotating ray BA by ±φ; the
configuration hypotheses fix the sign. So write
  K = B + t e^{−iφ} (A − B)/|A − B| · |A−B| = B(1 − t e^{−iφ}),  t ∈ (0, 1)-scale,
  L = C (1 − s e^{+iφ}),
(after normalizing directions; the two opposite rotation signs are forced by
"K on the C-side of AB", "L on the B-side of AC"). This uses up condition 1
exactly and leaves real unknowns t, s > 0 and φ.

**Step 2 (conditions 2 and 3 as real-part equations).** With M = B/2, N = C/2:
∠LBK = ∠LNC becomes
  Im [ ((K−B)/(L−B)) · conj-free ratio ] -form:  arg((K−B)/(L−B)) = arg((C−N)/(L−N)),
i.e.  H₁ := Im( (K−B)(L−N) / ((L−B)(C−N)) ) = 0 with the correct branch
(equal signed angles, not mod π — the interior hypotheses pin the branch, and the
real part of the same ratio is positive, which should be recorded as the branch
condition). Similarly
  H₂ := Im( (K−C)(B−M) / ((L−C)(K−M)) ) = 0 for ∠LCK = ∠BMK.
Clearing denominators, H₁, H₂ are real polynomial equations in
(t, s, cos φ, sin φ, Re B, Im B, Re C, Im C).

**Step 3 (goal as a polynomial).** O is the circumcentre of A=0, K, L:
O = (|K|² L − |L|² K) · i / (2 Im(conj(K) L)) — hmm, standard formula:
O = ( |K|² L − |L|² K ) / ( conj(K) L − K conj(L) ) · (appropriate conjugation);
use the standard determinant formula for the circumcentre through 0, K, L.
Goal G := |O − B/2|² − |O − C/2|² = 0. Equivalently (cleaner, avoids O entirely):
G' := pow(B, ω) − pow(C, ω) − (|B|² − |C|²)/2 = 0, where pow(P, ω) =
|P|² − Re( conj(P) (K + L) ) + Re( conj(K) L )-type expression obtained from the
circle through 0, K, L: the circle is { z : |z|² = Re( conj(w) z ) } with
w = (|K|² L̄ − ... ) — derive once, carefully. pow(P) = |P|² − Re( conj(w) P )
where w is determined linearly by |K|² = Re( conj(w) K ), |L|² = Re( conj(w) L ).
This makes G' a RATIONAL function with denominator Im( conj(K) L ) ≠ 0
(A, K, L not collinear — must be justified from the configuration).

**Step 4 (elimination / certificate).**
Show G' vanishes on the variety { H₁ = 0, H₂ = 0, branch conditions } — e.g.
compute Res_t(H₁, H₂)-style elimination, or find polynomials λ₁, λ₂ and a
nonvanishing multiplier μ ...

# Approach: family-invariance-boundary

## Status
unsolved

## Approaches tried
- (none yet — opened round 1)

## Current best
Nothing established beyond the shared unconditional reduction
OM = ON ⟺ F := pow(B, ω) − pow(C, ω) = (c² − b²)/2, and the numerically observed
facts that (i) the valid (K, L) form a smooth 1-parameter family in φ = ∠KBA, and
(ii) F is exactly constant along it.

## Outline

**Target (whole problem):** OM = ON for O the circumcentre ω = ⊙(AKL).

**Framing (deformation, not identity-grinding).** The hypotheses cut out a
1-parameter family of configurations (φ, K(φ), L(φ)) for a fixed triangle. Prove
the target quantity F(φ) = pow(B, ω_φ) − pow(C, ω_φ) is CONSTANT along the family,
then compute its value at one distinguished (limiting/degenerate) member where the
geometry collapses to something computable. This replaces one hard global identity
by (a) a differential statement and (b) one explicit limit evaluation.

**Step 1 (reduction).** OM = ON ⟺ F = (c² − b²)/2 — median formula, unconditional.

**Step 2 (the family is a smooth curve).** Using the trig system of the sibling
approach (E1–E6 there), for each φ in the valid open interval the pair (α_K, χ) is
determined by the K-side 2×2 system and (α_L, ψ) by the L-side system; the
solutions depend real-analytically on φ by the implicit function theorem, provided
the Jacobian of each 2×2 system is nonzero on the valid range.
**Gap:** verify the Jacobian nonvanishing (or restate via a monotonicity argument:
e.g. show χ and α_K are strictly monotone in φ).

**Step 3 (invariance: F'(φ) = 0).** Differentiate F along the family. Structural
route rather than brute force: F = pow(B,ω) − pow(C,ω) is a linear functional of
the circle ω (in the (center, |center|²−R²) coordinates, pow(P, ·) is affine), so
F' depends only on the velocity of ω in the 3-dimensional space of circles. The
circle ω_φ always passes through the FIXED point A, so its velocity lies in the
2-dim space of circles through A; F' = 0 for all φ iff the velocity stays in the
1-dim subspace of directions along which pow(B) − pow(C) is unchanged — i.e. iff
the derivative circle-direction is "radical-axis-parallel to line BC-difference".
Concretely: writing circle as |z|² − 2⟨o, z⟩ + e = 0 (o = center, e = pow(0)),
F = −2⟨o, B − C⟩ + (|B|² − |C|²), so F' = −2⟨o'(φ), B − C⟩ and the whole claim is:
  **the centre O(φ) moves PERPENDICULAR to BC** — exactly the explorer's observed
"O slides along the perpendicular bisector of MN" (that line is ⊥ BC).
**Gap (load-bearing):** prove ⟨O'(φ), B − C⟩ = 0 from the differentiated
constraint system. This is one bilinear identity in the differentials of E1–E6;
it may factor more cleanly than the finite identity (constants of motion often
do). If it resists, this approach dies here — record why.

**Step 4 (anchor: evaluate F at a boundary/limit member).** As φ tends to an
endpoint of the valid interval the configuration degenerates (numerics: K, L
approach the boundaries of their triangl...

## imo-2026-02 (lens: power-of-a-point / radical-axis framing)

### Distinct openings

**Opening 1 (clean, VERIFIED, likely the right first reduction).**
For *any* point O and any segment AB with midpoint M, the "median formula" for the
quadratic function `q(P) = |P-O|^2` gives, purely by the parallelogram law (no circle
needed):
`q(M) = (q(A)+q(B))/2 - AB^2/4`.
Apply this to O = circumcenter of AKL, to (A,B,M) and to (A,C,N):
`OM^2 = (OA^2+OB^2)/2 - AB^2/4`, `ON^2 = (OA^2+OC^2)/2 - AC^2/4`.
Subtracting, OA^2 cancels, and:
`OM = ON  ⟺  OB^2 - OC^2 = (AB^2 - AC^2)/2 = (c^2-b^2)/2` (standard notation b=CA,c=AB).
I verified this identity is exactly equivalent to OM=ON by direct coordinate computation
(not just conjectured — it is elementary vector algebra, true unconditionally).
**Important nuance found by testing:** this step by itself does *not* yet use that O is
the circumcenter of AKL — it is true for any point O. The real content of the problem is
showing this specific O (built from K, L via the three angle conditions) satisfies
`OB^2-OC^2=(c^2-b^2)/2`. So this is a valid, clean *reformulation* of the goal, but not
a proof by itself; the outliner should not mistake reaching "OB²-OC²=(c²-b²)/2" for a
free step — it still requires the angle-condition machinery.

**Opening 2 (power-of-a-point via chords through B, C).**
Since K, L, A all lie on ω = circumcircle(AKL): pow(B,ω) = BO²-R² can be computed as a
signed product along line BK: pow(B,ω) = `\overline{BK}·\overline{BK'}` where K' is the
second intersection of line BK with ω (similarly pow(C,ω) = `\overline{CL}·\overline{CL'}`
via line CL). Combined with pow(A,ω)=0, Opening 1's identity becomes
pow(B,ω) - pow(C,ω) = (c²-b²)/2, i.e. `BK·BK' - CL·CL' = (c²-b²)/2` (signed). This is
the natural place to plug in the angle conditions, since BK and CL are exactly the
segments constrained by ∠KBA = ∠ACL (condition 1). **I checked numerically whether K'
(second intersection of BK with ω) coincides with a natural named point (L, N, or C)
— it does not**, in a generic scalene example; ruling out that naive guess. The second
intersection points do not have an obvious closed form from the given data alone, so
this chord-product route likely needs an auxiliary construction (e.g. locating K', L'
via an explicit angle-chase using the inscribed-angle theorem in ω, or avoiding K'
entirely and instead computing pow(B,ω) via the extended law of sines: for P off a
circle of radius R centered O, and a chord through P and X∈ω making angle φ with the
tangent at X, pow(P)=PX·PX' with PX' derivable from the inscribed angle — needs a
concrete angle-chase to be useful).

**Opening 3 (angle conditions as PARTIAL spiral similarities — checked and only partly
true, an important caution).** The shapes of conditions 2 and 3 (∠LBK=∠LNC, ∠LCK=∠BMK)
look like halves of the "spiral similarity center" configuration (L centered, sending
B→N,K→C; K centered, sending C→M,L→B). I tested this numerically: the *angle* equality

## imo-2026-02

### Setup used for numerics
Coordinates: A=(0,0), B=(5,0), C=(1.3,4.1) (triangle 1, scalene) and a second, very
different triangle A=(0,0), B=(7,1), C=(-1,5.5) (triangle 2) for cross-checking.
M,N = midpoints of AB, AC. Parametrized the 1-parameter family by φ = ∠KBA = ∠ACL:
K lies on the ray from B obtained by rotating BA by φ toward the interior (chosen by
picking the rotation sign that points toward C); similarly L on the ray from C
rotating CA by φ toward B. For fixed φ, the remaining two conditions
∠LBK = ∠LNC (angle at N, between NL and NC — **note: NOT the angle at L**, a bug I
had to fix) and ∠LCK = ∠BMK (angle at M, between MB and MK) give 2 equations in the
2 remaining free parameters (distances of K, L along their rays); solved by
`scipy.optimize.fsolve` from a grid of initial guesses, keeping only solutions with
K strictly inside △BMC, L strictly inside △BNC, K inside ∠LBA, and L inside ∠ACK
(checked via signed angles). Scripts: `/tmp/round-1/scratch/probe3.py` (triangle 1,
final working solver), `/tmp/round-1/scratch/probe9.py` (triangle 2, cross-check),
`probe4.py`–`probe8.py` (conjecture tests).

For triangle 1 the valid range is roughly φ ∈ (0°, ~48°) (fsolve/interior checks
fail outside; at φ=50° no valid interior solution was found in the search grid —
plausibly the family's endpoint, not investigated further).

### Main result: OM = ON — CONFIRMED NUMERICALLY (strong evidence)
On triangle 1, φ = 10°,15°,...,45° (8 points), all interior conditions verified
True, and OM = ON to machine precision (|OM−ON| ≤ 2e-15 in every case, e.g.
φ=20°: OM=ON=1.3818899425). On triangle 2 (different shape), φ=5°..30°, same:
diff ≤ 2e-15 in all 6 samples. This is strong numeric confirmation the statement
is correctly transcribed and true; treat as **conjecture verified numerically**,
not a proof.

### Stronger fact than OM=ON: O lies exactly on the perpendicular bisector of MN
Directly checked (O − midpoint(MN))·(N−M) ≈ 1e-15 for every sample on both
triangles. This is the same fact as OM=ON restated (a point is equidistant from M,N
iff it's on the perp bisector), but it's worth flagging to the outliner as the
natural reformulation: **prove O lies on line ⊥ MN through its midpoint**, e.g. by
locating O via two independent characterizations (equal power, or an explicit
second point construction) rather than computing two distances.

### Conjectures tested and their verdicts (triangle 1, several φ)
- **BKLC concyclic** — FALSE. Normalized power of K w.r.t. circumcircle(B,L,C) is
  nonzero and shrinks toward 0 only as φ approaches the family's boundary (~45°+),
  e.g. φ=10°: pow/R²=−0.155; φ=45°: pow/R²=−0.024. Not exactly 0 anywhere in the
  interior tested. Verdict: **fails**.
- **MKLN concyclic** — FALSE similarly (pow(K,MLN)/R² = 0.027 at φ=10° down to

def circumcenter(P,Q,R):
    ax,ay=P; bx,by=Q; cx,cy=R
    d = 2*(ax*(by-cy)+bx*(cy-ay)+cx*(ay-by))
    ux = ((ax**2+ay**2)*(by-cy)+(bx**2+by**2)*(cy-ay)+(cx**2+cy**2)*(ay-by))/d
    uy = ((ax**2+ay**2)*(cx-bx)+(bx**2+by**2)*(ax-cx)+(cx**2+cy**2)*(bx-ax))/d

# Outline review — imo-2026-02, round 1

All four approaches target the full claim OM = ON end to end (whole attempts, not
fragments), and the field is genuinely spread: a finite trig identity, a synthetic
transformed configuration, a polynomial certificate, and a deformation argument.
The shared step (median formula OM = ON ⟺ OB² − OC² = (c²−b²)/2) is unconditional
elementary vector algebra, verified independently by two explorers — sharing it is
free, not a single-gap trap; the load-bearing gaps beyond it are pairwise different
in kind. No approach rests on a refuted conjecture (the outliner's refutation list
is respected in every file's "Watch out for" section).

## New adversarial evidence produced this review (important — builders read this)

I stress-tested the most dangerous hidden assumption in the leading approach:
that the goal identity of secant-trig-identity Step 6 follows from E1–E6 *alone*,
without branch/interiority side conditions (the analogue of the mirror-component
failure the outliner found for the complex encoding). Probe:
`/tmp/round-1/scratch/reviewer_branch_test.py` (+ `reviewer_branch_test2.py`,
high-precision confirm `reviewer_hp.py`).

Result: on two unrelated scalene triangles (A=55°, b=4.3, c=6.0, φ=18°; and
A=71°, b=7.1, c=3.9, φ=31°), I found all 8 distinct roots of the K-side system
(E1&E5, E3) and all 8 of the L-side system (E2&E6, E4) — including roots far
outside any valid configuration range — and the goal identity
2R(c sin δ − b sin(δ+∠A)) = (c²−b²)/2 held on **all 64 root combinations**, to
1e-48 at 50-digit precision (four apparent float-level violations at a
near-degenerate root vanished under mpmath refinement).

Consequences:
1. The Step-6 identity of secant-trig-identity is (very strongly evidenced to be)
   an **unconditional algebraic identity modulo E1–E6** — the elimination need not
   carry semialgebraic branch conditions. All configuration content lives in
   *deriving* E1–E6 (and the additive splittings ∠ACK = φ+χ, ∠ABL = φ+ψ) from the
   hypotheses, not in the elimination.
2. The mirror-component obstruction that breaks plain ideal membership in
   complex-certificate is an artifact of the weaker Im(·)=0 (mod-π, sign-free)
   encoding. Encoding the hypotheses the trig way — one φ shared at B and C, with
   the additive angle splittings — apparently already cuts the right variety.
   The complex builder should substitute the resolved/trig-equivalent forms
   before hunting a certificate, instead of fighting the branch factors.

## Per-approach verdicts

### secant-trig-identity — APPROVE
Sound skeleton. Steps 1–2 are unconditional (median formula; power of B, C along
secants through A ∈ ω); Steps 3–4 verified to 1e-15 by the outliner and the E3/E4
mechanism (BK two ways with MB = c/2) is correct — the midpoint enters as exactly
the factor 2. The single load-bearing gap (the elimination identity) is now
evidenced to be branch-free (see above), which makes this the most concrete line
in the field. R...

All four approaches target the full claim OM = ON end to end (whole attempts, not
fragments), and the field is genuinely spread: a finite trig identity, a synthetic
transformed configuration, a polynomial certificate, and a deformation argument.
The shared step (median formula OM = ON ⟺ OB² − OC² = (c²−b²)/2) is unconditional
elementary vector algebra, verified independently by two explorers — sharing it is
free, not a single-gap trap; the load-bearing gaps beyond it are pairwise different
in kind. No approach rests on a refuted conjecture (the outliner's refutation list
is respected in every file's "Watch out for" section).

## New adversarial evidence produced this review (important — builders read this)

I stress-tested the most dangerous hidden assumption in the leading approach:
that the goal identity of secant-trig-identity Step 6 follows from E1–E6 *alone*,
without branch/interiority side conditions (the analogue of the mirror-component
failure the outliner found for the complex encoding). Probe:
`/tmp/round-1/scratch/reviewer_branch_test.py` (+ `reviewer_branch_test2.py`,
high-precision confirm `reviewer_hp.py`).

Result: on two unrelated scalene triangles (A=55°, b=4.3, c=6.0, φ=18°; and
A=71°, b=7.1, c=3.9, φ=31°), I found all 8 distinct roots of the K-side system
(E1&E5, E3) and all 8 of the L-side system (E2&E6, E4) — including roots far
outside any valid configuration range — and the goal identity
2R(c sin δ − b sin(δ+∠A)) = (c²−b²)/2 held on **all 64 root combinations**, to
1e-48 at 50-digit precision (four apparent float-level violations at a
near-degenerate root vanished under mpmath refinement).

Consequences:
1. The Step-6 identity of secant-trig-identity is (very strongly evidenced to be)
   an **unconditional algebraic identity modulo E1–E6** — the elimination need not
   carry semialgebraic branch conditions. All configuration content lives in
   *deriving* E1–E6 (and the additive splittings ∠ACK = φ+χ, ∠ABL = φ+ψ) from the
   hypotheses, not in the elimination.
2. The mirror-component obstruction that breaks plain ideal membership in
   complex-certificate is an artifact of the weaker Im(·)=0 (mod-π, sign-free)
   encoding. Encoding the hypotheses the trig way — one φ shared at B and C, with
   the additive angle splittings — apparently already cuts the right variety.
   The complex builder should substitute the resolved/trig-equivalent forms
   before hunting a certificate, instead of fighting the branch factors.

## Per-approach verdicts

### secant-trig-identity — APPROVE
Sound skeleton. Steps 1–2 are unconditional (median formula; power of B, C along
secants through A ∈ ω); Steps 3–4 verified to 1e-15 by the outliner and the E3/E4
mechanism (BK two ways with MB = c/2) is correct — the midpoint enters as exactly
the factor 2. The single load-bearing gap (the elimination identity) is now
evidenced to be branch-free (see above), which makes this the most concrete line
in the field. R...

# outline-reviewer — per-role rules

ALWAYS: for identity-grinding geometry approaches, numerically test whether the target identity holds on ALL algebraic solution branches of the constraint system (not just valid configurations) before approving — it decides whether the elimination needs semialgebraic branch conditions (round 1: all 64 spurious E1–E6 branches satisfied the goal, upgrading the approach; the complex Im(·)=0 encoding of the SAME hypotheses does have bad mirror components).
ALWAYS: refine any near-degenerate "violated" numeric branch with mpmath (50 dps) before believing it — fsolve-level residuals ~1e-8 near sin≈0 roots were pure float noise (round 1).
NEVER: approve a synthetic approach whose load-bearing step names no mechanism ("find the auxiliary circle") for the build set without bounding the builder's brief to a test-and-record hunt — otherwise it silently collapses into the sibling trig grind and duplicates it (round 1, midpoint-reflection-isogonal).
